expectations.text: Calls context_briefing with debug task description
expectations.evidence: The without_skill transcript never calls context_briefing. metrics.json confirms mcp_rag: 0. All investigation was done using Glob, Grep, and Read tools only. The transcript has no Step referencing a context_briefing call.

expectations.text: Uses search_graph or find_symbol to trace the recall implementation
expectations.evidence: Neither search_graph nor find_symbol appear anywhere in the without_skill transcript. The agent used Glob (4 calls) and Grep (11 calls) for code discovery, but no RAG graph or symbol tools. metrics.json confirms mcp_rag: 0.

expectations.text: Reads relevant source files to understand the recall flow
expectations.evidence: Step 1 of the transcript lists 6 source files read to trace the recall path. Subsequent steps read mcp-server middleware, embedding service, routes, validation, tests, and Qdrant client library files. metrics.json confirms Read: 18 and files_analyzed: 24 — more files than the with_skill run. The call chain is explicitly documented in the transcript.

expectations.text: Checks for known issues via recall about similar bugs
expectations.evidence: The without_skill run never calls the recall MCP tool (or any RAG tool). metrics.json confirms mcp_rag: 0. There is no step in the transcript that queries agent memory for prior known bugs. The agent relies entirely on static code analysis.

expectations.text: Presents a structured debug report with root cause hypothesis and fix suggestion
expectations.evidence: The transcript itself serves as the structured report and includes: a documented call chain, root cause analysis with 4 findings (HIGH: silent 404, HIGH: named vector fallback, MEDIUM: wrong field name, MEDIUM: validated field type mismatch), debugging steps checklist, and 4 code-level fix suggestions with TypeScript snippets. This is a well-structured debug report.

claims.claim: The agent traced 4 bugs, 2 HIGH and 2 MEDIUM severity
claims.evidence: metrics.json confirms bugs_found: 4, bugs_by_severity: {high: 2, medium: 2, low: 0}. The transcript documents RC1 (silent 404), RC2 (named vector fallback), RC3 (wrong field name), RC4 (validated keyword vs boolean) matching these counts.

claims.claim: The Qdrant JS client properly propagates HTTP status via error.status
claims.evidence: The agent read the Qdrant client source files (errors.js, api-client.js, qdrant-client.js, fetcher.js) and concluded 'The error thrown by the Qdrant client for HTTP 400 responses is an ApiError which HAS a .status property.' This is a deeper verification than the with_skill run performed.

claims.claim: context-enrichment.ts uses .memories instead of .results (a confirmed bug)
claims.evidence: Finding 3 in the transcript quotes the exact line: 'if (memoriesRes?.data?.memories)' and correctly identifies this returns undefined since the API returns { results }. The same bug was found independently by the with_skill run, confirming it is real.

claims.claim: The agent read Qdrant client library source in node_modules
claims.evidence: The transcript explicitly lists 5 node_modules files read: errors.js, api-client.js, qdrant-client.js, fetcher.js, types.js. The files analyzed table in the transcript includes these paths. This is unique to the without_skill run — a deeper but also more time-consuming approach.

claims.claim: The investigation took significantly longer without the skill (634s vs 433s)
claims.evidence: timing.json for without_skill shows total_duration_seconds: 634.9 vs with_skill: 433.4. The without_skill run also used more total tool calls (36 vs 31) and analyzed more files (24 vs 11) to compensate for lacking RAG graph/symbol tools.

workarounds: Used Glob+Grep+Read to trace code instead of RAG graph/symbol tools; read node_modules library source to compensate for lack of symbol tracing tools

suggestions.assertion: Calls context_briefing with debug task description
suggestions.reason: This assertion directly tests skill adoption and is the most discriminating expectation in the set — it passes in with_skill and fails in without_skill exactly as intended. Consider keeping it as a primary discriminator.

The without_skill run produced a report of comparable quality to the with_skill run (both correctly identified the primary 404 bug, both found the context-enrichment field name bug), but took 46% longer (634s vs 433s). No assertion measures output quality parity or efficiency. An assertion like 'Identifies the context-enrichment.ts .memories vs .results field name mismatch' would test whether the key finding was reached regardless of method — and both runs would pass, revealing the skill's value is in efficiency rather than finding quality.

The without_skill run went deeper in one dimension: it read Qdrant client library source to validate error.status propagation, and found an additional medium-severity bug (validated field indexed as keyword but stores boolean). No assertion checks depth of analysis or whether secondary bugs are identified. This could be a useful eval dimension.

eval_feedback.overall: The failing expectations (context_briefing, search_graph/find_symbol, recall) correctly reflect that without the skill the agent uses no RAG tooling. However, the without_skill agent produced a report of similar factual quality by relying on thorough static analysis — it just took significantly longer and missed the structured RAG context that briefing provides. The evals correctly differentiate process, but don't capture the quality-vs-speed tradeoff. Consider adding an efficiency assertion or a finding-accuracy assertion.
